content.text: The researcher coordination and triage process is the operational procedure through which an organisation manages its relationship with security researchers who have submitted vulnerability reports. It covers everything that happens between the moment a report is received and the moment a remediated vulnerability is publicly disclosed: acknowledgement, completeness and scope assessment, severity classification, validation, communication cadence with the researcher, and handoff to the engineering team. The quality of this process determines whether the organisation's vulnerability disclosure programme (VDP) functions as a genuine security intelligence asset or merely as a compliance checkbox.

The triage process for agentic AI systems is more demanding than for traditional software because the vulnerability classes are less standardised. A security engineer triaging a classic web application vulnerability (SQL injection, XSS, authentication bypass) can apply well-established reproduction and severity frameworks. An engineer triaging a prompt injection in an agentic system must understand how the agent's tool access amplifies the impact, whether the injection requires social engineering or can be delivered programmatically, and whether the vulnerability is in the application layer, the orchestration layer, or the model itself — each of which has different remediation ownership and timelines.

Researcher coordination is the relationship management dimension of the process. Security researchers, particularly those contributing to a VDP without financial incentive, are volunteers who have invested their time in the organisation's security. The way they are treated during the coordination process determines whether they become repeat contributors or whether they become frustrated and choose to disclose publicly without coordination. Programmes with excellent researcher communication produce significantly more and higher-quality reports over time than programmes that treat researchers as report-generators rather than collaborators.

Without a defined coordination process, the triage function becomes inconsistent. Different security engineers handling different reports make different decisions about severity, scope, and communication — producing an inconsistent researcher experience that undermines the programme's credibility. A researcher who submits a valid Critical vulnerability and receives no response within a week may escalate to public disclosure, triggering an incident response that could have been a managed remediation. A clear process with defined SLAs, decision criteria, and communication templates ensures that the programme operates consistently regardless of which team member handles a given report.

The triage quality directly affects the organisation's security posture. A triage process that incorrectly classifies a Critical vulnerability as Medium because the engineer did not account for the agentic tool access amplification will route that vulnerability to a 90-day remediation queue rather than a 7-day emergency fix. For agentic systems with broad tool access, a misclassified Critical finding may result in a significant incident before the fix is deployed. Investing in triage quality — trained engineers, AI-specific severity criteria, peer review for Critical findings — has a direct payoff in avoided incidents.

A defined coordination workflow ensures that every report receives the same sequence of handling steps regardless of the handler, producing a consistent researcher experience and a complete audit trail.
Structured triage criteria, including agentic-specific impact factors, ensure that vulnerability severity is assigned based on actual exploitability and impact rather than surface-level assessment.
Timely, transparent communication with researchers builds the trust that turns one-time reporters into repeat contributors and programme advocates in the security community.
Researchers who receive timely acknowledgement and regular updates are unlikely to resort to uncoordinated public disclosure; those who feel ignored or stonewalled are much more likely to.

A documented triage trail — acknowledgement timestamps, scope decisions, severity classifications, researcher communications — constitutes evidence of systematic vulnerability management for regulatory review.

Every submitted report requires human assessment. A public programme generating hundreds of reports per month requires dedicated triage staff or a third-party platform to avoid an SLA backlog that degrades the researcher experience.

Reproducing agentic AI vulnerabilities often requires access to the same model version and configuration in which the researcher encountered the issue. Model non-determinism and version differences can make reproduction inconsistent, complicating triage.

Researchers may dispute out-of-scope decisions, particularly for agentic systems where the boundary between application code and model behaviour is unclear. Handling disputes requires a defined escalation path and consistent criteria.

Well-known vulnerability classes (prompt injection, jailbreaking) generate many duplicate reports, each requiring triage. Automation can detect duplicates but requires investment in duplicate detection infrastructure.

The triage process follows a defined decision tree. When a report arrives, an automated system sends an acknowledgement including a case number, the programme SLAs, and a reminder of the safe harbour terms. The report is automatically checked for completeness using a structured template: does it include a clear description of the vulnerability, reproduction steps, the affected system or endpoint, and the researcher's assessment of impact? Incomplete reports trigger an automated request for the missing information, with a deadline for the researcher to respond before the report is closed.

Complete reports proceed to scope assessment. A security engineer reviews the reported system against the programme's scope definition. For agentic systems, this includes confirming whether the reported vulnerability is in an in-scope component (the application's orchestration layer, tool APIs, input handling, or model prompt pipeline) or in out-of-scope infrastructure (the underlying model provider, third-party APIs, or customer data stores). Out-of-scope reports are closed with a clear explanation, and if the vulnerability affects a third party, the reporter may be directed to that party's own VDP.

In-scope reports proceed to validation and severity assessment. The security engineer attempts to reproduce the vulnerability using the researcher's steps. For agentic AI vulnerabilities, reproduction may require constructing a sandboxed instance of the agent environment to avoid executing adversarial inputs against production systems. Once reproduced, the engineer applies the severity assessment framework, including agentic impact adjusters, and assigns a severity tier. A second engineer peer-reviews all Critical and High severity classifications before they are finalised.

The validated finding is communicated to the researcher along with the organisation's severity assessment. The communication should acknowledge the researcher's work, confirm the finding is valid and in-scope, state the severity classification with a brief rationale, and provide the remediation SLA target. From this point, the researcher receives status updates at defined intervals — typically weekly for Critical, bi-weekly for High, monthly for Medium — until the fix is deployed and the disclosure coordination begins.

attrs.alt: Researcher coordination and triage decision flow: report received, auto-ACK, completeness check, scope check, severity assessment, and remediation handoff

content.text: Reports flow through two sequential decision gates before reaching severity assessment: completeness (does the report contain enough information to triage?) and scope (is the reported system within the programme's defined scope?). Incomplete reports route to a researcher information request; out-of-scope reports are closed with explanation. In-scope complete reports proceed to severity assessment, researcher notification, and remediation handoff, with a defined update cadence maintained throughout remediation.

A formal triage process is needed from the moment the VDP accepts its first report. Even a private programme with a small number of trusted researchers requires a documented process to ensure consistent handling. The process complexity should scale with report volume: a private programme receiving two or three reports per month can operate with a lightweight checklist and manual tracking; a public programme receiving hundreds of reports per month requires a platform, automation, and dedicated triage staff.

Define the minimum information a valid report must contain: vulnerability description, affected endpoint or component, reproduction steps, observed and expected behaviour, and researcher's impact assessment. Provide this template in the VDP policy to guide researcher submissions.

Set up an automated response that sends within the SLA window, includes a case number, confirms receipt, states the next expected communication timeline, and reminds the researcher of the safe harbour terms.

Create a scope decision reference that maps system components to in-scope/out-of-scope status. For agentic systems, this must address: which tool integrations are in scope, whether the underlying model provider infrastructure is out of scope, and what the boundary is between application behaviour and model behaviour.

Document the severity assessment criteria for AI-specific vulnerability types. For each class (prompt injection, tool abuse, context leakage, orchestration auth bypass), define what constitutes Critical, High, Medium, and Low, considering the agent's tool access and autonomous action scope.

Establish an isolated instance of the agentic system for vulnerability reproduction — one that mirrors the production configuration but is disconnected from production data and external systems. Document the process for provisioning and using this environment.

Create standard templates for each communication type: validation acknowledgement, out-of-scope closure, severity verdict, remediation status update, and disclosure coordination request. Templates ensure consistency while allowing personalisation.
Implement peer review for Critical and High:

Require a second security engineer to review severity assessments of Critical and High findings before communicating them to the researcher or handing off to engineering. This prevents both underclassification (missing urgency) and overclassification (creating false urgency).
Monitor acknowledgement and verdict communication SLAs in real time. Alert triage managers when a report is at risk of an SLA breach before it occurs, not after.
A researcher communication template for sending the validated severity verdict:
A triage state machine in code:

from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Optional


class ReportState(str, Enum):
    RECEIVED = "received"
    ACKNOWLEDGED = "acknowledged"
    AWAITING_INFO = "awaiting_info"
    IN_TRIAGE = "in_triage"
    OUT_OF_SCOPE = "out_of_scope"
    DUPLICATE = "duplicate"
    NOT_REPRODUCIBLE = "not_reproducible"
    VALIDATED = "validated"        # Severity assigned, handoff to engineering
    REMEDIATION = "remediation"    # Fix in progress
    PENDING_DISCLOSURE = "pending_disclosure"
    CLOSED = "closed"


@dataclass
class VDPReport:
    case_id: str
    researcher_id: str
    submitted_at: datetime
    state: ReportState = ReportState.RECEIVED
    severity: Optional[str] = None
    agentic_factors: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    state_history: list[tuple] = field(default_factory=list)

    def transition(self, new_state: ReportState, actor: str, note: str = "") -> None:
        """Transition the report to a new state, recording the change."""
        valid_transitions = {
            ReportState.RECEIVED: [ReportState.ACKNOWLEDGED],
            ReportState.ACKNOWLEDGED: [ReportState.IN_TRIAGE, ReportState.AWAITING_INFO],
            ReportState.AWAITING_INFO: [ReportState.IN_TRIAGE, ReportState.CLOSED],
            ReportState.IN_TRIAGE: [
                ReportState.VALIDATED,
                ReportState.OUT_OF_SCOPE,
                ReportState.DUPLICATE,
                ReportState.NOT_REPRODUCIBLE,
            ],
            ReportState.VALIDATED: [ReportState.REMEDIATION],
            ReportState.REMEDIATION: [ReportState.PENDING_DISCLOSURE],
            ReportState.PENDING_DISCLOSURE: [ReportState.CLOSED],
        }
        if new_state not in valid_transitions.get(self.state, []):
            raise ValueError(f"Invalid transition from {self.state} to {new_state}")
        self.state_history.append((self.state, new_state, actor, datetime.now(timezone.utc), note))
        self.state = new_state


# Usage
report = VDPReport(
    case_id="VDP-2024-0143",
    researcher_id="researcher@example.com",
    submitted_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc),
)
report.transition(ReportState.ACKNOWLEDGED, actor="automation", note="Auto-ACK sent")
report.transition(ReportState.IN_TRIAGE, actor="sec-eng-1", note="Complete report, in scope")
report.severity = "critical"
report.agentic_factors = ["autonomous_irreversible_actions", "tool_api_abuse"]
report.transition(ReportState.VALIDATED, actor="sec-eng-2", note="Peer review confirmed Critical")

A technology company's VDP receives 47 reports in the first month following the public launch of its agentic AI assistant programme. The triage team reviews the incoming volume and finds: 18 duplicates of a known low-severity prompt injection finding that had already been identified and was in the remediation queue; 12 out-of-scope reports targeting the underlying model provider's API; 9 incomplete reports requiring more information from the researcher; 6 invalid reports describing expected model behaviour as a vulnerability; and 2 valid in-scope findings.

Of the 2 valid findings, one describes a Medium-severity context window issue in which a crafted user input could cause the agent to include previous conversation fragments in subsequent unrelated responses — a privacy concern but with limited exploitability. The second describes a Critical-severity orchestration layer authentication bypass that allows an attacker who can make HTTP requests to the agent's internal API to invoke tool actions without an authenticated user session, potentially enabling the agent to be directed to read and exfiltrate files from any user's cloud storage.

The Critical finding is peer-reviewed by a second security engineer who confirms the severity classification. The engineering lead is notified immediately and the finding is escalated to the incident response process. The researcher receives a communication within 4 hours of the report being validated, acknowledging the finding, confirming its severity, and committing to a 7-day remediation target. The fix — adding authentication enforcement to all internal tool dispatch endpoints — is deployed within 5 days. The researcher validates the fix against the sandbox environment, and coordinated disclosure is scheduled for 30 days post-fix.

A report targeting a third-party AI model provider's infrastructure should be closed with a pointer to that provider's VDP — simply saying 'out of scope' without guidance damages the researcher relationship and leaves a potential vulnerability unreported.
Testing prompt injection payloads or adversarial inputs in the production agentic environment risks executing the exploit against real user data and systems. Always reproduce in an isolated sandbox.

Without a documented severity rubric and peer review, the same vulnerability class can be rated differently by different engineers depending on their AI security experience, creating inconsistent researcher expectations and remediation prioritisation.

Failing to send status updates during a long remediation window causes researchers to assume their report has been ignored or deprioritised. Even a brief 'update: fix is in progress, target date unchanged' prevents frustration and keeps researchers from going public.

LLM-based agents are non-deterministic. A researcher who found a prompt injection that works 30% of the time may have a valid finding that appears not to reproduce in triage. Establish a sufficient number of reproduction attempts before declaring 'not reproducible.'

Communicate more than the minimum. Researchers who receive unprompted positive updates ('we are making good progress on the fix') have measurably better programme satisfaction than those who only receive responses when they ask.

Acknowledge when the researcher caught something the team missed. A brief 'this is a finding we had not identified internally — thank you' is highly valued by researchers and reinforces the programme's collaborative culture.

Document all triage decisions in the case management system with the rationale, not just the outcome. This creates an institutional knowledge base for future triage decisions and enables consistent handling of similar reports.

Allow researchers to validate fixes before closing the report. This confirms that the fix addresses the root cause rather than just the specific reproduction path, and gives the researcher ownership in the remediation outcome.

Conduct quarterly triage retrospectives. Review triage accuracy (were severity classifications upheld by engineering?), SLA performance, and researcher satisfaction metrics to identify and address process weaknesses before they become programme-level failures.

The primary performance metric for the triage process is SLA compliance rate — the percentage of reports for which each defined SLA (acknowledgement, verdict, status updates) is met on time. Best-in-class programmes maintain above 95% SLA compliance across all report categories. When SLA compliance falls below 90%, it indicates triage capacity is insufficient for report volume, and remediation options include: adding triage staff, implementing automation for completeness and duplicate detection, or adjusting programme scope to reduce invalid report volume.

Automation provides significant triage scaling benefits. Automatic duplicate detection (hashing report content and matching against open cases) eliminates the need for a human to review duplicates until a unique finding is confirmed. Automatic completeness checking against the submission template flags incomplete reports before human triage begins, saving engineer time. For programmes operating on third-party platforms, these automation features are typically built in.
End-to-end case management: intake, duplicate detection, SLA tracking, researcher communication, state machine
Engineering handoff ticket creation; remediation tracking; integration with VDP platform via webhook
SLA breach alerts; Critical severity escalation to on-call security engineering
Isolated reproduction of agentic AI vulnerabilities; mirrors production configuration without production data or live tool connections
Excellent — but requires vendor onboarding on agentic AI context

The triage environment used to reproduce vulnerabilities must be isolated from production. An engineer who pastes a prompt injection payload into a production AI assistant to 'test it quickly' may trigger tool actions that affect real users or data. Enforce a policy that all vulnerability reproduction occurs in the designated sandbox environment, and that sandboxes are reset between reproductions to prevent contamination from previously tested payloads.

Case management system access must be controlled. The triage platform contains sensitive information about unpatched vulnerabilities — if this information were accessed by an attacker before remediation, they would have advance knowledge of exploitable weaknesses. Limit access to active vulnerability reports to the triage and engineering teams directly responsible for handling them, with additional access controls on Critical and High severity reports.

The researcher coordination process determines whether a VDP produces genuine security intelligence or merely processes report volume. Process quality directly affects researcher relationship quality and, consequently, report quality over time.
Two sequential decision gates — completeness and scope — filter incoming reports before engineer-intensive severity assessment begins, concentrating skilled triage effort on valid in-scope findings.
Agentic AI vulnerability reproduction must be performed in an isolated sandbox. Testing adversarial inputs against production systems risks executing the exploit and causing real harm.

Severity classification for agentic vulnerabilities requires an AI-specific rubric that accounts for the agent's tool access and autonomous action scope — standard CVSS scoring without adjustment will systematically underclassify findings.
Peer review of Critical and High severity classifications prevents both over- and under-classification, ensuring that remediation urgency matches the actual risk.
Regular communication during remediation — even brief status updates — prevents researchers from escalating to public disclosure out of frustration.
SLA compliance rate is the primary operational metric. Below 90% compliance signals insufficient triage capacity and must be addressed before it damages researcher trust and programme reputation.
